abstract:
Copper-based catalysts are frontline candidates for converting CO2 into methane via electrolysis, yet achieving high selectivity remains a formidable challenge due to the complex, multi-step nature of the reaction. In this work, we propose a dual-level design strategy that optimizes both the atomic structure of the active sites and the mesoscale architecture of the carbon support to steer the reaction pathway toward methane.

By utilizing the microphase separation of a PAN-b-PMMA block copolymer, we synthesized a carbon fiber support characterized by an interconnected network of uniform mesochannels. These bicontinuous channels create a unique local microenvironment: they are inherently water-deficient and CO2-rich, which effectively suppresses the competing hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Furthermore, the long-range confinement of these pores increases the residence time of CO intermediates, facilitating their deep reduction to methane.

Crucially, operando investigations using in situ X-ray absorption and Raman spectroscopy reveal a dynamic structural evolution under reaction conditions. We found that the initial atomically dispersed Cu-N3 sites undergo a reversible reconstruction into nitrogen- and hydroxyl-stabilized Cu3 clusters (N,OH-Cu3). Theoretical calculations confirm that these newly identified N,OH-Cu3 clusters possess a moderate affinity for *CO and significantly lower the energy barrier for *CO hydrogenation, while simultaneously hindering the C–C coupling pathway that leads to multicarbon products.

As a result of this synergistic multiscale engineering, the catalyst achieves a remarkable methane Faradaic efficiency of 74.2% at an industry-relevant current density of 300 mA cm−2. This research provides a powerful framework for navigating complex interfacial catalytic pathways by integrating atomic-level site design with mesoscale transport control.
